title: Semantic Chunking
one_liner: Split documents at embedding-detected topic boundaries instead of fixed character counts, so each chunk is a semantically coherent unit.
problem_solved: Fixed-size (character/token) splitters cut documents at arbitrary points, slicing sentences and ideas mid-thought. The resulting chunks mix unrelated topics or omit the sentence that completes a fact, which muddies their embeddings and returns incoherent or partially relevant passages at retrieval time.
failure_modes: My retrieved chunks cut off mid-sentence or mid-idea, so the answer's supporting text is split across two chunks
Fixed-size chunks blend two unrelated topics, giving muddy embeddings and retrieval that returns only partially relevant text
No single chunk size/overlap setting works across my heterogeneous documents — every value fragments something
Retrieved passages read as incoherent fragments and the LLM answers from incomplete context
Long flowing prose (reports, papers, legal text) has no headings to split on, so structural splitters fall back to arbitrary cuts
when_to_use: Your corpus is long, discursive prose (scientific papers, legal documents, comprehensive reports) where facts depend on the surrounding sentences
Documents lack reliable structural markers (headings, sections, markup) that a structure-aware splitter could use
You have evidence that fixed-size splitting is fragmenting facts across chunk boundaries and hurting retrieval relevance
You want better chunk boundaries without any LLM calls — only an extra embedding pass at index time
Chunk coherence matters more to you than predictable chunk sizes
tradeoffs: Extra embedding API calls for every sentence at index time — higher index build cost and latency, scaling with corpus size
Chunk sizes become variable and unpredictable: some chunks can exceed your context budget, others can be a single sentence; often needs a max-size fallback splitter
The breakpoint threshold (percentile / standard_deviation / interquartile and its amount) must be tuned per corpus; a bad threshold over- or under-splits
Ignores explicit document structure — if your docs have good headings or markup, a structure-aware splitter (or contextual chunk headers) may beat it more cheaply
Lives in langchain_experimental, so the API is less stable than core splitters
Does nothing for retrieval failures caused by query-vocabulary mismatch, multi-hop questions, or tabular/visual content — it only fixes boundary placement
how_it_works: The notebook reads a PDF into one raw string via a repo helper (read_pdf_to_string, built on PyMuPDF/fitz), then instantiates LangChain's SemanticChunker from langchain_experimental with OpenAIEmbeddings, breakpoint_threshold_type='percentile' and breakpoint_threshold_amount=90. The splitter embeds consecutive sentences, computes the semantic distance between each adjacent pair, and places chunk boundaries wherever the distance exceeds the 90th percentile of all distances (standard_deviation and interquartile are alternative threshold strategies). text_splitter.create_documents([content]) produces variable-length, topically coherent Document chunks. These are then embedded again with OpenAIEmbeddings and indexed into a FAISS vector store (indexing stage), and a retriever with k=2 serves a test query ('What is the main cause of climate change?') through the repo's retrieve_context_per_question helper. The technique operates purely at chunking/indexing time — query-time behavior is a plain similarity-search retriever, so it composes freely with any retrieval or post-retrieval technique.
key_dependencies: PyMuPDF (fitz) via repo helper_functions for PDF-to-string extraction
related_techniques: simple_rag: refines (drop-in replacement for the fixed-size splitter in a baseline RAG pipeline)
choose_chunk_size: alternative (tunes fixed-size splitting instead of replacing it)
proposition_chunking: alternative (LLM-generated atomic propositions vs embedding-boundary chunks; more precise but far more expensive)
contextual_chunk_headers: composes (prepend document/section context to whatever chunks semantic chunking produces)
context_enrichment_window_around_chunk: alternative (fixes boundary fragmentation at retrieval time by expanding to neighboring sentences instead of at chunking time)
relevant_segment_extraction: composes (reconstructs longer multi-chunk segments at query time from coherent base chunks)
contextual_compression: composes (post-retrieval trimming pairs well with variable-size semantic chunks that can run large)
summary: Semantic chunking replaces fixed-size text splitting with boundary detection based on embedding similarity between adjacent sentences. The notebook reads a PDF into a raw string, then applies LangChain's SemanticChunker (langchain_experimental) with OpenAI embeddings: consecutive sentences are embedded, distances between neighbors are computed, and a split is placed wherever the distance exceeds a statistical threshold — percentile (used here at 90), standard deviation, or interquartile. The resulting variable-length, topically coherent chunks are embedded with OpenAIEmbeddings and indexed in a FAISS vector store; a k=2 retriever serves queries. It addresses failures where fixed character/token splitters cut sentences or ideas mid-thought, producing chunks that mix topics or lose the sentence completing a fact, degrading both embedding quality and answer grounding. Best for long discursive documents (reports, papers, legal text) lacking reliable structural markers. Costs: an extra embedding pass over every sentence at index time, unpredictable chunk sizes (some oversized, some tiny), per-corpus threshold tuning, and no awareness of explicit structure like headings or tables.
notes_for_chunking: Structure: one giant front-loaded markdown overview cell (cell-1) contains essentially the entire method description (overview, motivation, breakpoint types, benefits, conclusion) — it is self-contained and should be the primary text chunk. After it, the notebook is a sequence of tiny single-purpose code cells (1-3 lines each) preceded by short '###' markdown headers ('Define file path', 'Read PDF to string', 'Split original text to semantic chunks', 'Create vector store and retriever', 'Test the retriever'); each header+code pair is only meaningful as a unit, so keep header and following code together. One mid-notebook markdown cell usefully documents the three breakpoint_threshold_type options — high signal, keep with the SemanticChunker instantiation cell. Junk to strip: cell-0 book-promo/marketing block, pip install cell, git-clone + sys.path boilerplate, wget data-download cell (contains a duplicated wget line), image-embed cell referencing ../images/*.svg, and the trailing tracking-pixel markdown cell. No stored outputs or base64 blobs. Caveat for code-as-ground-truth: 'from helper_functions import *' hides where FAISS, read_pdf_to_string, retrieve_context_per_question, and show_context come from — resolve against the repo-root helper_functions.py.
